meta.title: Latticework — sources & attribution
meta.about: The sources behind Latticework's catalog, structured for attribution. Munger is the seed of the catalog; these are the primary texts his models were mined from and the secondary compilations that were audited for provenance. Every URL here was actually fetched during research and verified against the research record — none reconstructed from memory.

types.skeptical: Skeptical — critiques of the mental-models genre

meta.note: The raw research artifacts (fan-out workflow outputs, ~1.9MB) are working scratch and were not committed; this is the curated, verified extract. Model-level citations live on each entry in latticework-catalog.json (mungerCitation / mungerQuote / listedBy).

sources.title: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk One: Harvard School Commencement Speech
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Poor Charlie's Almanack opening talk; primary chapter of the canon, cited for Munger's inversion heuristic applied to life advice.
sources.attributionNote: Talk One of Poor Charlie's Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman — Munger's Harvard School (Los Angeles) commencement address, 1986, the well-known inversion talk on how to guarantee a life of misery.

sources.title: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Primary source for the '80 or 90 important models will carry 90% of the freight' figure, the origin of the 'latticework' metaphor, and Munger-named math/business/microeconomics models (compound interest, permutations & combinations, Fermat/Pascal, decision trees, competitive destruction, surfing, circle of competence, pari-mutuel markets).
sources.attributionNote: USC Business School, 1994. Originally published as a transcript in Outstanding Investor Digest (May 5, 1995) and reprinted in the valueplays 'Best of Charlie Munger' PDF; Farnam Street hosts this full-text copy.

sources.title: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment — 1995 Harvard speech ('24 Standard Causes of Human Misjudgment')
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: The 1995 speech; cited for the '24 Standard Causes' framing, item-one incentives (reinforcement/incentives, FedEx night-shift example), the coined 'man-with-a-hammer syndrome,' and the lollapalooza-effect Q&A.
sources.attributionNote: June 1995 Harvard speech, presented as the '24 Standard Causes of Human Misjudgment'; the Whitney Tilson transcription actually enumerates only 22 items with bracketed corrections. Distinct edition from the 2005 rewrite (separate entry). Alternate full transcript at singjupost.com.

sources.title: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk Three: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, Revisited
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Book edition of the 1996 Stanford talk; cited for restating the latticework tied back to USC, the ~100-model target, and a large set of Munger-named psychology models plus the 'use them as a checklist' method.
sources.attributionNote: Talk Three of Poor Charlie's Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman — the canonical book edition of Munger's Stanford Law School 1996 talk. Reproduces the material first published as the Outstanding Investor Digest Dec 1997 transcript (separate entry).

sources.title: Worldly Wisdom Revisited (Stanford Law School, 1996) — Outstanding Investor Digest transcript
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: First-generation transcript of the 1996 talk; cited for the 'best 100 or so models' target and its discipline list, lollapalooza effects, critical mass (physics) and autocatalysis (E.O. Wilson), Jacobi's 'invert, always invert,' and the physiology/Huxley biology models.
sources.attributionNote: Stanford Law School, April 19, 1996. First-generation transcript published in Outstanding Investor Digest, Dec 29, 1997 (pp. 24-31); scanned PDF archived at mjbaldbard.wordpress.com. Same talk later reproduced as Talk Three of Poor Charlie's Almanack (separate entry).

sources.title: The Best of Charlie Munger: 1994-2011 (Berkshire & Wesco annual-meeting notes)
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Meeting-notes compilation; cited for Munger declining to enumerate a '99 mental models' list (2011 Wesco), the private 'little list' of microeconomic models, and reprinted 1994 USC/OID worldly-wisdom material.
sources.attributionNote: Compilation of Berkshire Hathaway and Wesco Financial annual-meeting notes, 1994-2011, compiled by Whitney Tilson / Yanan Ma Bledsoe (some sections transcribed by Peter Boodell); hosted on valueplays.net.

sources.title: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment — 2005 revision (25 Tendencies; Talk Eleven of Poor Charlie's Almanack)
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: The only edition naming all 25 numbered Tendencies (Reward and Punishment Superresponse through Lollapalooza); the version any 'all 25 biases' catalog must cite.
sources.attributionNote: Munger's 2005 rewrite, 'made from memory unassisted by any research,' later Talk Eleven of Poor Charlie's Almanack; names all 25 numbered Tendencies. Farnam Street publishes this full text with Munger's written permission. Distinct edition from the 1995 speech (separate entry).

sources.title: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting — Buffett & Munger Q&A (session transcripts)
sources.creator: Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Annual-meeting Q&A used for ad-hoc mental-model illustrations in Munger's own words alongside Buffett.
sources.attributionNote: Video and synchronized transcripts hosted and maintained by CNBC's Warren Buffett Archive (buffett.cnbc.com). Cited Munger-relevant sessions run 2000, 2004, 2008 (afternoon), 2013 and 2018 (morning/afternoon sessions); the archive itself covers meetings 1994-2023. Content is the joint Buffett-Munger Q&A.

sources.title: Daily Journal Corporation Annual Meeting 2013 — Notes of Charlie Munger's Remarks
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Earliest Daily Journal meeting in the corpus; Munger's remarks on mental models and rationality.
sources.attributionNote: Notes of Munger's remarks compiled and maintained by Worldly Partners as part of their Charlie Munger Archive (https://worldlypartners.com/charlie-munger-archive/). Kept separate from the Latticework Investing Daily Journal transcripts because it is a distinct maintainer and year.

sources.title: Daily Journal Corporation Annual Meeting — Charlie Munger Q&A (full transcripts)
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Late-life restatements of Munger's mental models — e.g. 'denial' named as the dominant cause of human bad decisions and the Demosthenes 'what people wish is what they believe' quote at the 2023 meeting.
sources.attributionNote: Full annual-meeting transcripts maintained by Latticework Investing across 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022 and 2023 at this domain (same URL pattern, one page per year); the 2023 transcript shown is the representative link. The 2023 Q&A is independently mirrored at kingswell.io and steadycompounding.com.

sources.title: Charlie Munger — Harvard-Westlake School Talk
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: 2010 school talk cited for Munger's practical-wisdom and psychology models.
sources.attributionNote: Only located copy is a user-uploaded PDF on pdfcoffee.com — a low-authority host with no named transcript maintainer; flagged accordingly. This is a prepared talk grouped here among the late speeches/interviews.

sources.title: A Conversation with Charlie Munger at University of Michigan Ross
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Cross-disciplinary worldly-wisdom themes and rationality-as-moral-duty in a late-life conversation.
sources.attributionNote: Transcript maintained by Worldly Partners in their Charlie Munger Archive (https://worldlypartners.com/charlie-munger-archive/).

sources.title: Charlie Munger at Caltech (interview transcript)
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Late-life (2020) interview corroborating Munger's worldly-wisdom and opening-remarks themes.
sources.attributionNote: Transcript prepared and maintained by Empire Financial Research (transcript dated 12-18-20).

sources.title: Charlie Munger — Acquired podcast interview
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: One of Munger's last long-form interviews, on his investing and thinking philosophy.
sources.attributionNote: Munger interviewed by Acquired hosts Ben Gilbert and David Rosenthal; episode page hosts the audio and show notes.

sources.title: Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom (CNBC)
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: 2023 CNBC special/interview transcript corroborating late-life restatements of Munger's models.
sources.attributionNote: Full transcript published by CNBC (Nov 30, 2023) for its 'Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom' program.

sources.title: Investing: The Last Liberal Art (orig. 'Latticework: The New Investing')
sources.creator: Robert G. Hagstrom
sources.supports: Foundational book-length latticework elaboration; the source of many non-Munger discipline models later amplified by others.
sources.attributionNote: First published as 'Latticework: The New Investing' (Texere, 2000); reissued as 'Investing: The Last Liberal Art' (2001); substantially revised 2nd ed., Columbia Business School Publishing, 2013 (adds a Mathematics chapter and Kahneman/Stanovich/Tetlock-updated Decision Making). Earliest book-length elaboration of Munger's latticework and a major channel through which non-Munger models (equilibrium, complex adaptive systems, pragmatism) entered the genre. Hagstrom does not claim the disciplines are Munger's: Ch.1 recounts Munger's 1994 USC talk, then presents his OWN liberal-arts/Great Books selection (St. John's College reading list reprinted as an appendix). Provenance audit: 54 items, 7 direct-Munger. No book-catalog URL in the vetted set — see the CFA Institute review entry for the linkable reference.

sources.title: Seeking Wisdom: From Darwin to Munger
sources.creator: Peter Bevelin
sources.supports: 49-item misjudgment-and-thinking-tools compilation in the Munger/Darwin tradition.
sources.attributionNote: Post Scriptum AB, 2003 (3rd ed. 2007). Structure: 28 psychological reasons for misjudgment, 9 physics/math causes, 12 thinking tools; appendix reproduces Munger's 1986 Harvard School commencement address plus Munger/Buffett quotes. Bevelin does not claim the lists are Munger's — preface: 'The ideas in it are built largely from the works and thoughts of others.' Part Two's 28 reasons reorganize and expand Munger's 24 'standard causes of human misjudgment' (~17 of 28 map directly to Munger). Provenance audit: 49 items, 27 direct-Munger. No canonical/citable URL for the book appears in the vetted source set, so url is intentionally empty.

sources.title: Charlie Munger: The Complete Investor
sources.creator: Tren Griffin
sources.supports: Book-length Munger synthesis; its 25iq companion supplies the 165-item model list audited for this catalog.
sources.attributionNote: The book deliberately omits a model list; the discipline-by-discipline ~100-model list ('A partial list of some Mental Models used by Charlie Munger, compiled from many sources') lives in Griffin's companion 25iq.com post 'A Dozen Things I've Learned from Charlie Munger about Mental Models and Worldly Wisdom' (Aug 22, 2015) — no verbatim URL for that post exists in the vetted source set. Griffin explicitly frames the compilation as his own: Munger 'has never prepared a complete list covering all disciplines.' Provenance audit (book + 25iq): 165 items, 63 direct-Munger.

sources.title: Charlie Munger Mental Models (Full 129 List + Latticework Guide)
sources.creator: J.D. Meier
sources.supports: 129-item Munger mental-model compilation with an accompanying latticework guide.
sources.attributionNote: Title ('Full 129 List') and heading ('Charlie Munger's 129 Mental Models') imply canonicity, but the body hedges: 'Some are Munger's own labels; others are standard models he explicitly and repeatedly used or endorsed,' and its own Q&A concedes Munger said he used '80 or 90 pretty well.' Presented as Meier's Munger-inspired curation, not Munger's own list. Provenance audit: 129 items, 60 direct-Munger.

sources.title: Farnam Street Mental Models (master list of ~100 models)
sources.creator: Shane Parrish (Farnam Street)
sources.supports: Largest secondary latticework taxonomy (~100 models across 8 disciplines); the main channel expanding Munger's mental-model canon.
sources.attributionNote: Site named after Berkshire Hathaway's street address, with Munger cited as its inspiration. Presents 98 top-level models in 8 discipline buckets as Farnam Street's OWN curation ('The Big Ideas From The Big Disciplines'), fronted by a single Munger latticework epigraph — not offered as Munger's own list. Content mirrors Parrish's 'The Great Mental Models' book series. Provenance audit: 101 items checked, 26 direct-Munger, 24 plausible, 51 community additions. (Farnam Street's separate PHM transcript is published with Munger's written permission — that copy is cataloged in the primary group.)

sources.title: Charlie Munger's Tendencies of Human Misjudgment
sources.creator: Novel Investor
sources.supports: Convenient reference list/summary of Munger's tendencies of human misjudgment.
sources.attributionNote: Secondary reference summary of the tendencies from Munger's 'The Psychology of Human Misjudgment.' The authoritative full transcript — published with Munger's written permission — is Farnam Street's (primary group); this is a summary, not a source of record.

sources.title: Farnam Street's Great Mental Models
sources.creator: Ravi Nagarajan (The Rational Walk)
sources.supports: Third-party review of the Farnam Street mental-models compilation.
sources.attributionNote: Independent review/discussion of Farnam Street's 'The Great Mental Models,' situating the FS compilation within the Munger latticework genre.

sources.title: Full Transcript: The Psychology of Human Misjudgement — Charlie Munger
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Alternate full-text PHM transcript (reference copy).
sources.attributionNote: Full-text transcript of Munger's PHM speech, maintained by Singju Post. The authoritative copy — published with Munger's written permission — is Farnam Street's (primary group); this is an alternate full transcript included for reference.

sources.title: Munger's Latticework
sources.creator: ModelThinkers
sources.supports: Concise explainer of the latticework-of-mental-models concept.
sources.attributionNote: Platform explainer of Munger's latticework concept; a secondary reference, not a Munger source.

sources.title: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment by Charlie Munger (Sloww summary)
sources.creator: Kyle Kowalski (Sloww)
sources.supports: Detailed reference summary of PHM; cross-used in the Bevelin audit.
sources.attributionNote: Detailed secondary summary of Munger's PHM; also used as a consulted source in the Bevelin 'Seeking Wisdom' provenance audit. Authoritative transcript is Farnam Street's (published with Munger's permission).

sources.title: Book Review: Investing: The Last Liberal Art
sources.creator: CFA Institute (Enterprising Investor)
sources.supports: Authoritative third-party review situating and validating the Hagstrom latticework book.
sources.attributionNote: Third-party review of the 2013 2nd edition of Hagstrom's book on CFA Institute's Enterprising Investor blog. Serves as the linkable reference for the Hagstrom compilation, which has no catalog URL in the vetted source set. Reviewer not named in the inputs; credited to the publishing blog.

sources.title: 24 Cognitive Biases — Charlie Munger's Standard Causes of Human Misjudgment
sources.creator: Barry Ritholtz
sources.supports: Quick-reference list of Munger's 24 cognitive biases (1995 numbering).
sources.attributionNote: Reference list of Munger's 24 cognitive biases on Ritholtz's 'The Big Picture' blog. Reflects the 1995 '24 Standard Causes' numbering; the fuller 25-tendency version is Munger's own 2005 Poor Charlie's Almanack rewrite.

sources.title: A Framework for Putting Mental Models to Practice, Part 1
sources.creator: Cedric Chin
sources.supports: Skeptical, practice-oriented critique of the mental-model compilation approach.
sources.attributionNote: Companion to 'The Mental Model Fallacy'; argues for practice-based acquisition over list-memorization of Munger-style models. Skeptical / attribution-audit angle.

sources.title: The Mental Model Fallacy
sources.creator: Cedric Chin
sources.supports: Skeptical counterweight on the value and provenance of mental-model lists.
sources.attributionNote: Skeptical / attribution-audit critique arguing that memorizing lists of mental models (the Munger-genre compilations) is a weak substitute for tacit, experience-derived knowledge — a deliberate counterweight to the compilations above.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Four: Practical Thought About Practical Thought?
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: The turning-$2M-into-$2T Coca-Cola thought experiment; Pavlovian/operant conditioning, autocatalysis, social proof, the five helpful notions.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Five: The Need for More Multidisciplinary Skills from Professionals
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Harvard Law School 50th reunion, 1998; multidisciplinary worldly wisdom, man-with-a-hammer, the four-discipline hard-science ethos, tragedy of the commons, invisible hand/foot.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Six: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Croupier’s take / frictional costs, overconfidence, indexing, buried covariance, focus investing, self-insurance.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Seven: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Galbraith’s bezzle and Munger’s febezzle/foolexures extension, wealth effect, Keynesian multiplier, crowd folly.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Eight: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Fictional Quant Tech accounting parable; functional-equivalence test, dollop-by-dollop gradualism, Ponzi/chain-letter mechanics, reputational rub-off.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Nine: Academic Economics — Strengths and Faults
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: UCSB Herb Kay Memorial Lecture, 2003; the nine faults — fatal unconnectedness, physics envy, febezzlement, comparative advantage & second-order effects, Gresham’s law extension, opportunity cost, multidisciplinarity.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: Learning machine, inversion, the iron prescription, seamless web of deserved trust, assiduity, Planck vs chauffeur knowledge, avoiding envy/resentment/self-pity.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack, Chapter Two: The Munger Approach to Life, Learning, and Decision Making
sources.creator: Charlie Munger
sources.supports: The Munger Approach plus the Investing Principles Checklist and Mungerisms; two-track analysis, circle of competence, moats, margin of safety, opportunity cost, sit-on-your-ass investing, EBITDA critique.
sources.attributionNote: Poor Charlie’s Almanack (Stripe Press digital edition), edited by Peter D. Kaufman.
